"Forgiveness" II Corinthians 2:5-11
description: Preaching from 2 Corinthians 2:5-11, this sermon examines the subject of forgiveness within the church, using the Apostle Paul's counsel to the conflicted church at Corinth as the framework for understanding how believers are to forgive, comfort, and restore one another.

notes.introduction: Preaching from 2 Corinthians 2:5-11, this sermon examines the subject of forgiveness within the church, using the Apostle Paul's counsel to the conflicted church at Corinth as the framework for understanding how believers are to forgive, comfort, and restore one another.
outline: I. The Conflict in Corinth (vv. 5-6) — An offender in gross immoral sin (referenced in 1 Corinthians 5) had been punished by the church; though he repented, he had not been restored, and some in the church resisted restoration, creating ongoing conflict that kept the church from doing the will of God.

II. Paul's Counsel (vv. 7-10) — A three-step plan for the church: (1) Forgive him; (2) Comfort him — come alongside to encourage and strengthen; (3) Confirm your love toward him. Paul tests their obedience, noting they obeyed in judgment and must equally obey in forgiveness and restoration. The goal of church discipline is restoration, not excommunication.

III. Paul's Concern (v. 11) — Failure to forgive gives Satan an advantage; Paul warns that unforgiveness leads to bitterness and division, and that the church must not give place to the devil by leaving the matter unresolved.
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by declaring forgiveness a pillar of biblical Christianity, reminding the congregation that God's grace is sufficient to do what God has commanded. He prayed for any unsaved to come to salvation, and for those with an unforgiving spirit to be helped by God to forgive, comfort, and confirm their love — being biblical in practice, with restoration as the goal.
notes.application: Believers who have been offended are to: (1) forgive the offender, (2) comfort the offender by coming alongside to encourage and strengthen them, and (3) confirm their love toward the offender — not stopping at step one. Do not let anger simmer; deal with it and do not give place to the devil. Remember that you yourself have been forgiven by God, which should move you to forgive others. If you are holding an unforgiving spirit, recognize that bitterness destroys the one who holds it and gives Satan an advantage over the church.

"King Of Kings And Lord Of Lords" I Timothy 6:13-21
description: Preaching from 1 Timothy 6:13-21, the pastor delivers a closing charge from Paul's first letter to Timothy, showing how three truths about God — his power, his position, and his plan — motivate believers to continue running the Christian race and finish strong.

notes.introduction: Preaching from 1 Timothy 6:13-21, the pastor delivers a closing charge from Paul's first letter to Timothy, showing how three truths about God — his power, his position, and his plan — motivate believers to continue running the Christian race and finish strong.
outline: I. God's Power — He quickeneth all things; he alone gives life and has power over death (vv. 13, 16); no power exists except what is given from above (John 19:11)
II. God's Position — He is the blessed and only potentate, the King of kings and Lord of lords (v. 15); thy God reigneth and is on his throne regardless of earthly chaos

III. God's Plan — Keep the commandment until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ (v. 14); Jesus is coming again (John 14, Acts 1:11, 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18); God is not slack concerning his promise and is longsuffering, not willing that any should perish (2 Peter 3)
notes.conclusion: The pastor called the congregation to fix their attention on the King of kings and Lord of lords — the one they are running for — and to remember his power, his position, and his plan as motivation to continue, press on, and finish strong. He closed in prayer asking God to help believers keep their eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of their faith, and to encourage any who are struggling or overwhelmed to continue.
notes.application: Remember God's power when you are weak and want to give up. Remember his position — that thy God reigneth — and rest in that rather than being unsettled by chaos. Remember his plan and look for the appearing of the Lord Jesus Christ. Keep your eyes on Jesus, the one you are running for, and purpose to finish your course with joy. If you are not sure of heaven, trust the Lord Jesus Christ as your personal Savior.

"Overcome Evil With Good" I Thessalonians 5:15
description: An exposition of 1 Thessalonians 5:15, the eighth lesson in a series on the closing instructions of that letter, examining what it means to overcome evil with good rather than rendering evil for evil.

notes.introduction: An exposition of 1 Thessalonians 5:15, the eighth lesson in a series on the closing instructions of that letter, examining what it means to overcome evil with good rather than rendering evil for evil.
outline: I. A Principle of Grace — 'See that none render evil for evil unto any man' (1 Thess. 5:15a): The word 'see' carries the idea of 'take heed'; the flesh desires to react and get even, but believers are to be gracious and respond rather than react, following the example of Christ who when reviled, reviled not again (1 Pet. 2:21-23)

II. A Pursuit of Good — 'But ever follow that which is good, both among yourselves and to all men' (1 Thess. 5:15b): 'Follow' carries the idea of pursuing after; God, not society or culture, determines what is good; as Jesus 'went about doing good' (Acts 10:38), believers are to pursue that which is upright and honorable both within the church and toward all men
notes.conclusion: The preacher summed up both principles by saying they amount to one thing: being like Jesus. He referenced John 15:1-5 to stress that without Christ believers can do nothing, and that abiding in the vine is the only means of bearing fruit. He closed by calling the church to die to self, be filled with the Spirit, and follow the example Christ left in 1 Peter 2 — overcoming evil with good as David did when he refused to take vengeance on Saul. He closed in prayer asking God to help the congregation walk in the Spirit and live like Jesus.
notes.application: Die to self and be filled with the Holy Spirit, recognizing you cannot live the Christian life in your own power. Do not render evil for evil to any man — take heed of the flesh's desire to react and get even. Pursue that which is good as God defines it from His Word, not as society or culture defines it. Let your speech always be with grace (Col. 4:6). Look to God's Word to determine what is good and live accordingly, rather than following what everyone else is doing.

"The Lord Was With Joseph - Part 1" Genesis 39:1-6
description: A study of Genesis 39:1-6 examining the repeated statement 'the Lord was with Joseph' and what it meant for Joseph to live in the presence of God while serving as a slave and overseer in Potiphar's house in Egypt.

notes.introduction: A study of Genesis 39:1-6 examining the repeated statement 'the Lord was with Joseph' and what it meant for Joseph to live in the presence of God while serving as a slave and overseer in Potiphar's house in Egypt.
outline: I. Joseph the Overseer (Genesis 39:1-6) — the Lord was with Joseph, Potiphar observed it, and God's blessing was upon all that Joseph did

II. The Presence of God: Comforting and Convicting — 'the Lord was with Joseph' appears four times in Genesis 39; God's presence brings comfort (Psalm 23, Hebrews 13, Matthew 28) but also should convict and shape behavior
III. Potiphar's Observation — an unsaved Egyptian saw that the Lord was with Joseph, raising the question of whether others can see the Lord with us (Acts 4:13)
IV. Joseph's Service as Overseer — Joseph served cheerfully and wholeheartedly, was promoted to overseer over all Potiphar had, and God blessed Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake
V. New Testament Principles for the Believer's Work (Ephesians 6:5-6; Colossians 3:22-23) — do all work as unto Christ, not with eye service as men pleasers, but heartily as to the Lord
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by noting that Joseph's next appearance in the chapter will be as 'the accused,' and that even there Joseph kept his eyes on God. He stated that God's blessing and favor do not come by accident or random chance but come as we humbly follow the Lord in obedience to His Word. He also cautioned that what looks like a blessing may be a curse and vice versa, and that we need God's perspective rather than the world's. He then prayed that the congregation would serve as unto Christ, that others would see the difference of Christ in their lives, and that God's blessing and favor would be upon them.
notes.application: Live daily in keen awareness that the Lord is with you, because that awareness will impact the way you live. Work in whatever position God has placed you — as unto Christ, not as men pleasers and not with eye service — doing it heartily and wholeheartedly so that your testimony at work reflects the Lord. Seek to have a life that others can observe and say 'the Lord is with them.' Walk in obedience to God's Word in order to have God's blessing and favor upon your life.

"Jacob Prepares To Meet Esau" Genesis 32:1-23
description: Message 71 in a verse-by-verse study of Genesis, this sermon from Genesis 32:1-23 examines Jacob's preparation to reunite with his brother Esau after 20 years of separation, tracing his actions, his prayer, and his attempt to appease Esau with a large present.

notes.introduction: Message 71 in a verse-by-verse study of Genesis, this sermon from Genesis 32:1-23 examines Jacob's preparation to reunite with his brother Esau after 20 years of separation, tracing his actions, his prayer, and his attempt to appease Esau with a large present.
outline: I. The Preparation of Jacob (vv. 1-8) — Jacob sends messengers to Esau with a humble message, referring to Esau as 'my lord' and himself as 'thy servant'; upon hearing Esau is coming with 400 men, Jacob is greatly afraid and distressed, and divides his company into two bands

II. The Prayer of Jacob (vv. 9-12) — Jacob prays to the God of Abraham and Isaac, acknowledging he is unworthy of God's mercies, recalling God's specific command to return and God's promise to deal well with him, and asking God to deliver him from Esau's hand

III. The Present for Esau (vv. 13-23) — After praying, Jacob prepares an enormous gift of livestock sent in successive droves ahead of him to appease Esau; he then rises at night and sends his wives, servants, and eleven sons over the ford Jabbok to protect them
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by noting that though Jacob prayed, he still kept his own hand in the situation by preparing the present — something the congregation can relate to. He pointed forward to Genesis 32:24-32, where God meets Jacob alone, and to the reconciliation with Esau in chapter 33, as evidence that God can work in hearts no human effort or gift can reach. He urged the congregation to trust God, leave matters with Him, and remember that God is able. He closed in prayer asking God to help the people trust and obey Him rather than taking matters into their own hands.
notes.application: The preacher explicitly called for the following: (1) When fear comes, take it to the Lord as Jacob did — be transparent with God about your fears. (2) In moments of real or perceived adversity, do not forget God's leadership and do not forget God's promises. (3) When you pray and cast your burden on the Lord, actually leave it with Him rather than trying to keep your own hand in the situation. (4) Trust God to do what only He can do, because He is able.

"O How Love I Thy Law" Psalm 119:97
description: Preaching from Psalm 119:97, this sermon calls believers to love, learn, and live the Scriptures, arguing that the Bible is a treasure more precious than gold that every follower of Jesus Christ should cherish, study, and obey.

notes.introduction: Preaching from Psalm 119:97, this sermon calls believers to love, learn, and live the Scriptures, arguing that the Bible is a treasure more precious than gold that every follower of Jesus Christ should cherish, study, and obey.
outline: I. As a follower of Jesus, I should LOVE the Scriptures — Psalm 119:97, 140, 159, 163, 165, 167: the psalmist repeatedly declares love for God's Word, and that love should be proven by behavior, not merely claimed

II. As a follower of Jesus, I should LEARN the Scriptures — through (1) personal devotions and Bible study (1 Peter 2:2; 2 Timothy 2:15), (2) faithful church attendance where the Word is preached (Acts 20:28; 1 Timothy 4:6; 1 Peter 5:2), and (3) discipleship (Matthew 28:18-20); the more we love the Word the more we learn it, and the more we learn it the more we love it

III. As a follower of Jesus, I should LIVE the Scriptures — Joshua 1:8 commands meditating day and night in order to do all that is written, with the result of true prosperity and success; Psalm 19:7-11 shows the Word warns and rewards obedience; Psalm 86:11 expresses the desire to walk in God's truth; 2 Peter 1:19 presents the Word as a light in a dark place that we do well to heed
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by asking whether each person could stand with the psalmist and say 'I love the Word of God — I love it exceedingly,' with that love producing daily devotion, learning, and living of the Scriptures. He prayed that the Spirit would move each individual to examine their devotion life and church attendance, and extended an invitation to any present who had never trusted Christ as Savior.
notes.application: Maintain a daily personal devotion and Bible study time — do not rely solely on church services for spiritual sustenance. Attend church faithfully so as not to miss the spiritual meals provided through preaching and teaching. Get involved in discipleship with humility, acknowledging the need to learn God's Word. Go to the Word daily asking what God is showing you to apply and how to be a doer of the Word, not a hearer only.

"Deceiving Your Own Selves" James 1:22
description: Preached from James 1:22, this sermon warns against self-deception that comes from hearing the Word of God without doing it, drawing on the concluding illustration of Matthew 7 (the wise and foolish builders) to show the difference between hearers and doers.

notes.introduction: Preached from James 1:22, this sermon warns against self-deception that comes from hearing the Word of God without doing it, drawing on the concluding illustration of Matthew 7 (the wise and foolish builders) to show the difference between hearers and doers.
outline: I. The Message Delivered — the Sermon on the Mount was delivered perfectly by Jesus Christ Himself (Matthew 7:24, 26)
II. The Message Received — the message was heard by both groups; the problem was not with delivery or reception
III. The Message Applied (or Not) — the single difference between the wise man and the foolish man is 'doeth them' versus 'doeth them not,' determining whether the house stood or fell
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by urging the congregation not to deceive themselves, stating that the worst deception is self-deception and that we often do not even know when we are doing it. He called on God in prayer to help each person be a doer of the Word, not a hearer only, and expressed the desire to respond to the Spirit with 'yes, Lord, yes.'
notes.application: Come to church asking how God will speak to you personally, not hoping the message will fix someone else. In daily devotions, open the Word and ask specifically: What is God teaching me? What do I need to stop doing? What do I need to start doing? Respond to the invitation at the end of services rather than shying away from it. Commit to being a doer of the Word so that you are not deceiving your own self.

"What About Dinosaurs?" Genesis 1:24-25
description: Preached as lesson 51 in a series through Genesis, this sermon addresses the question 'What About Dinosaurs?' using Genesis 1:24-25 as its anchor text, examining what the Bible says about the origin, coexistence with man, and eventual extinction of dinosaurs.

notes.introduction: Preached as lesson 51 in a series through Genesis, this sermon addresses the question 'What About Dinosaurs?' using Genesis 1:24-25 as its anchor text, examining what the Bible says about the origin, coexistence with man, and eventual extinction of dinosaurs.
outline: I. Common Teaching About Dinosaurs (what the world says): dinosaurs evolved from reptiles and reigned 165 million years; destroyed by an asteroid 65 million years ago; humans and dinosaurs never coexisted; the word 'dinosaur' does not appear in the Bible

II. Where Did Dinosaurs Come From? God created them — beast of the earth after his kind (Genesis 1:24-25, day six); some possibly on day five (Genesis 1:20-23, water and flying creatures); the word 'dinosaur' was coined by Sir Richard Owen in 1841 and first published in 1842, so its absence from the Bible is not an argument against biblical authority

III. Did Humans and Dinosaurs Live at the Same Time? Yes — man was created on the same day six (Genesis 1:26-27); God gave man dominion over every living thing; behemoth in Job 40:15-24 matches description of a large sauropod, not a hippo or elephant; the word 'dragon' appears in Scripture as further evidence

IV. What Happened to the Dinosaurs / Why Are There So Many Fossils? The global flood (Genesis 6-8) killed all creatures not on the ark (Genesis 7:21-23); Noah took dinosaurs onto the ark — likely adolescent pairs, approximately 60 pairs needed (Genesis 7:7-9); dinosaurs came off the ark (Genesis 8:15-19); post-flood extinction explained by drastic climate change, reduced lifespan, vegetation changes, the Ice Age, and changed human-animal interaction

V. Historical Evidence for Post-Flood Dinosaurs: Alexander the Great's record of hissing dragons in India (326 BC); Marco Polo's descriptions of animals in his travels; the ancient Egyptian Narmer Palette depicting humans with long-necked creatures; a Stegosaurus carving on a Cambodian temple wall predating any Stegosaurus fossil discovery by 800 years; the Chinese Zodiac featuring 11 known animals plus a dragon; a 2017 fossil found off Norway's coast with 48 plant varieties preserved in its stomach, best explained by rapid burial in the global flood
notes.conclusion: The preacher summarized the three main answers — God made dinosaurs, humans and dinosaurs absolutely lived at the same time, and the global flood accounts for the fossils and eventual extinction — and closed in prayer asking God to strengthen the congregation's confidence in His Word, noting that starting and ending with the Bible provides real answers and that believers do not need to shy away from conversations about dinosaurs.
notes.application: Do not shy away from or avoid conversations with people about dinosaurs. Start with the Bible and end with the Bible when engaging the subject. Parents should be cautious about what the world teaches children about dinosaurs, because evolutionary teaching about millions of years can undermine the authority of the Word of God on other subjects. Be equipped to answer that the word 'dinosaur' not appearing in the Bible is not a valid argument, since the word was not coined until 1841.

"I Love To Tell The Story" Romans 1:14-16
description: A midweek sermon from Romans 1:14-16 in which the preacher uses Paul's three statements — 'I am debtor,' 'I am ready,' and 'I am not ashamed' — to call believers to recognize the privilege, priority, and power of telling the gospel story.

notes.introduction: A midweek sermon from Romans 1:14-16 in which the preacher uses Paul's three statements — 'I am debtor,' 'I am ready,' and 'I am not ashamed' — to call believers to recognize the privilege, priority, and power of telling the gospel story.
outline: I. Recognize the Privilege (v. 14 — 'I am debtor') — God has allowed us to be put in trust with the gospel; it is a privilege to be bearers of the good news, as Paul states in 1 Corinthians 9:16-17 and 1 Thessalonians 2:4

II. Make It a Priority (v. 15 — 'I am ready') — Paul's manner was to go immediately to where people were assembled and preach Christ; the church at Thessalonica likewise sounded out the word (1 Thessalonians 1:7-8); we must plan and look for opportunities

III. Recognize the Power (v. 16 — 'I am not ashamed') — the gospel is the power of God unto salvation; Paul's own conversion in Acts 9 and his testimony in Galatians 1:22-23 demonstrate that the gospel changes lives
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by tying all three points together — the gospel is a privilege, must be a priority, and is powerful — and prayed that God would help the congregation take the gospel to every creature and see many souls saved that year.
notes.application: Grab a handful of gospel tracts from the church lobby and distribute them. Take John and Romans booklets to share with neighbors. Sit down after the message, ask the Lord to help you devise a plan for getting the gospel out, and make witnessing a scheduled priority in your life. Participate in the church's organized outreach to reach the community.

FBC Oakton, VA - August 23, 2020 (Evening Worship)
description: A verse-by-verse study of Genesis 12:1-9, tracing Abram's journey from Haran to Canaan and examining the instruction God gave him, the promises of the Abrahamic Covenant, and the record of Abram's obedience summarized in the three words 'So Abram departed.'

notes.introduction: A verse-by-verse study of Genesis 12:1-9, tracing Abram's journey from Haran to Canaan and examining the instruction God gave him, the promises of the Abrahamic Covenant, and the record of Abram's obedience summarized in the three words 'So Abram departed.'
outline: I. The Instruction Received (v. 1) — God had spoken to Abram while still in Ur (cf. Acts 7:1-4); Haran was not the final destination; upon Terah's death God renewed the call to leave and go to the land He would show him.

II. The Promises Rehearsed (vv. 2-3) — Unconditional 'I will' promises of the Abrahamic Covenant: (1) I will make of thee a great nation; (2) I will bless thee and make thy name great; (3) thou shalt be a blessing; (4) I will bless them that bless thee and curse him that curseth thee; (5) in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed — fulfilled through the seed, Jesus Christ (Gen. 22:18; Gal. 3:6-18).

III. Abram's Obedience Recorded (vv. 4-9) — Abram departed at age 75 with Sarai, Lot, and all their substance; traveled to Shechem/plain of Moreh where the Lord appeared and promised the land to his seed; Abram built an altar and worshiped; moved to the mountain between Bethel and Ai, built another altar, and called upon the name of the Lord; journeyed on still toward the south (cf. Heb. 11:8-10).
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by returning to the three words 'So Abram departed,' holding them up as a model of faith and obedience to God's direction. He noted that believers today have the complete written Word of God rather than audible speech, and that the same simple response of trusting and obeying is required. He drew a parallel to the morning message — knowing access is available but not entering in is foolish — and applied it to obeying Scripture. He closed in prayer asking God to help the congregation obey His Word, worship Him in spirit and in truth, recognize His hand of blessing, and extended an invitation for any unsaved person to receive eternal life through Jesus Christ.
notes.application: The preacher explicitly called for the following: (1) Recognize that God is the source of blessing in your life and never credit yourself — 'Who gave you the ability to work?'; (2) Be absolutely assured you are following the direction God has given, not making decisions from a merely temporal human perspective; (3) Worship God on His terms, in spirit and in truth, not on man's terms; (4) Simply trust God and obey His Word as Abram did — 'So Abram departed'; (5) Any person not sure of heaven should receive the gift of eternal life through Jesus Christ before leaving or logging off.

title: "Prayer As Our First Response" I Timothy 2:1-8
"More With Us Than With Them" II Kings 6:8-23
"Be Ye Reconciled To God" II Corinthians 5:14-21

"As Ye Have Been Taught"  Colossians 2:6-8
description: Preaching from Colossians 2:6-8, this sermon uses the phrase 'as ye have been taught' as a springboard to address the church's responsibility to disciple and invest in the next generation, warning that if the church will not disciple people, the world will.

notes.introduction: Preaching from Colossians 2:6-8, this sermon uses the phrase 'as ye have been taught' as a springboard to address the church's responsibility to disciple and invest in the next generation, warning that if the church will not disciple people, the world will.
outline: I. The Example — Jesus taught with authority (Matthew 7:29; John 6:59; 7:28), regularly teaching truth to the next generation, and left us an example to follow his steps

II. The Engagement — Every believer must personally engage in teaching a future generation, as seen in Barnabas and Saul teaching at Antioch (Acts 11:19-26), Paul's charge to the Philippians (Philippians 4:9), and Paul's instruction to Timothy to commit truth to faithful men who will teach others also (2 Timothy 2:2)

III. The Examination — We must examine what we are teaching the next generation, asking: Do we teach them the Scriptures and the authority of God's Word? Do we teach them to walk with God? Do we teach them the importance of church? Do we teach them to give?
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed by urging the congregation to see the window of opportunity they have with the children in the church, to be purposeful and intentional about investing in a future generation, and to examine and adjust what is being taught so that when we say 'walk as ye have been taught,' we are certain we have taught them how they should walk. He closed in prayer asking God to help the church be doers of the word and to be intentional in investing in the next generation.
notes.application: Examine what you are teaching the next generation and ensure it is right teaching from God's Word. Teach children the Scriptures and the authority of the Bible. Teach children to walk with God and provide them spiritual guidance rather than leaving them to figure it out on their own. Teach children the importance of church attendance and make church a priority. Teach children to give by involving them in the offering. If you have no children at home, invest in and be a godly example to the children in the church. Be purposeful and intentional about discipling a future generation.

"I Want To Be Found Faithful"  II Timothy 4:8
description: Preaching from 2 Timothy 4:8, this sermon calls believers to pursue faithfulness to God's calling throughout their lives so they may receive the crown of righteousness at Christ's appearing.

notes.introduction: Preaching from 2 Timothy 4:8, this sermon calls believers to pursue faithfulness to God's calling throughout their lives so they may receive the crown of righteousness at Christ's appearing.
outline: I. Reaching Precedes Grasping — Paul's commitment to knowing and serving Christ, seen in his three statements: 'I have fought a good fight,' 'I have finished my course,' and 'I have kept the faith' (2 Tim. 4:6-7)

II. Obeying Precedes Rewarding — The crown is not earned by superior human effort but by faithful obedience to the specific calling and path God has given each believer, as God the righteous judge watches and keeps track

III. Occupying Precedes Appearing — Until Christ returns, every believer must use the gift God has given them in His service and guard against the world's distractions, as illustrated by the warning example of Demas (2 Tim. 4:10)
notes.conclusion: The preacher closed with the story of Colonel John B. Gordon holding the center line at Antietam despite five wounds, using it to illustrate that men give everything for causes that will not last. He charged the congregation that servants of the Lord have the opportunity to focus all they have on following God's path for them, so that when they stand before Christ and receive the crown of righteousness, they can cast it at His feet. His final call was for every hearer to purpose in their heart to be found faithful to the very end, no matter the wounds, difficulties, or distractions.
notes.application: Appreciate, protect, and participate in the church God has given you. Do more church, not less, as you see the day approaching. Make the focus of your life pursuing Jesus Christ, not what the world offers. Use the specific gift God has given you and occupy — keep using it in His service until He comes or calls for you. Guard your focus carefully; if something is distracting you or your family from the Lord, be willing to put it down. Purpose in your heart to be found faithful, and ask the Holy Spirit to search your heart and show you where you have lost focus so you can bring it back to the Lord.
